content:
{
  "title": "Fact-Check Chain-of-Thought: Prompting LLMs with Inline Source Verification for Reduced Hallucination",
  "problem": "Large language models frequently generate hallucinated or inaccurate factual content, especially on knowledge-intensive tasks, even with chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLM factuality is critical for real-world deployment in sensitive domains, as users increasingly rely on LLM outputs for decisions. Existing prompting methods either insufficiently constrain the model's factual grounding or introduce cumbersome external fact-checking.",
  "method": "We propose Fact-Check Chain-of-Thought (FC-CoT) prompting: each step of a CoT reasoning trace is paired with an explicit inline instruction for the model to provide a verifiable source or confidence rating for that specific fact. The prompt template enforces ('Claim:' ... 'Source/Confidence:') after every assertion, nudging the LLM to self-verify as it reasons. At inference, a lightweight retriever is optionally used to check model-supplied sources for consistency, but the core mechanism is in-prompt self-verification.",
  "experiment_plan": "The method will be evaluated on factual QA and long-form generation benchmarks (e.g., TriviaQA, HotpotQA, FEVER, TruthfulQA). Baselines include standard CoT and Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Metrics will include factuality (via human and automated scoring), hallucination rate, and source attribution accuracy. The main experiment is a head-to-head comparison of FC-CoT vs. CoT on blind factuality and hallucination evaluation."
}
